infobox.value: Paul Antschel  (1920-11-23)23 November 1920  Cernăuți, [Kingdom of Romania](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania") (now [Chernivtsi](/wiki/Chernivtsi "Chernivtsi"), Ukraine)

([/ˈsɛlæn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English");[[1]](#cite_note-1) German: [[ˈtseːlaːn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German"); born **Paul Antschel**; 23 November 1920 – c. 20 April 1970) was a German-speaking Romanian[[2]](#cite_note-2) poet, [Holocaust survivor](/wiki/Holocaust_survivor "Holocaust survivor"), and [literary translator](/wiki/Literary_translation "Literary translation"). He adopted his pen name (an anagram of the Romanian spelling **Ancel**) following the war and resided in France from 1949, becoming a naturalized French citizen in 1955.

(*The Extended Hours*), Celan (whose work had received only six votes) said "After the meeting, only six people remembered my name".[*[This quote needs a citation](/wiki/Wikipedia:Inline_citation#When_you_must_use_inline_

**Paul Celan** ([/ˈsɛlæn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English");[[1]](#cite_note-1) German: [[ˈtseːlaːn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German"); born **Paul Antschel**; 23 November 1920 – c. 20 April 1970) was a German-speaking Romanian[[2]](#cite_note-2) poet, [Holocaust survivor](/wiki/Holocaust_survivor "Holocaust survivor"), and [literary translator](/wiki/Literary_translation "Literary translation"). He adopted his pen name (an anagram of the Romanian spelling **Ancel**) following the war and resided in France from 1949, becoming a naturalized French citizen in 1955.

Celan is regarded as one of the most important figures in [German-language literature](/wiki/German-language_literature "German-language literature") of the post-[World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") era and a poet whose verse has gained an immortal place in the literary pantheon. Celan's poetry, with its many radical poetic and linguistic innovations, is characterized by a complicated and cryptic style that deviates from poetic conventions.

## Life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Celan&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life")]

### Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Celan&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early life")]

Celan was born into a German-speaking [Jewish](/wiki/Jew "Jew") family in [Cernăuți](/wiki/Cern%C4%83u%C8%9Bi "Cernăuți"), [Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina"), a region then part of Romania and earlier part of the [Austro-Hungarian Empire](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Empire "Austro-Hungarian Empire") (when his birthplace was known as Czernowitz). His first home was in the Wassilkogasse in Cernăuți. His father, Leo Antschel, was a [Zionist](/wiki/Zionism "Zionism") who advocated his son's education in [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language") at the Jewish school *Safah Ivriah* (meaning *the Hebrew language*). Celan's mother, Friederike (Fritzi) Antschel née Schrager, was an avid reader of [German literature](/wiki/German_literature "German literature") who insisted [Austrian German](/wiki/Austrian_German "Austrian German") be the language of the household. In his teens, Celan became active in Jewish [Socialist](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism") organizations and fostered support for the [Republican](/wiki/Second_Spanish_Republic "Second Spanish Republic") cause in the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War"). His earliest known poem is titled *Mother's Day 1938*.[[3]](#cite_note-google.com-3)

Paul attended the Liceul Ortodox de Băieți No. 1 (Boys' Orthodox Secondary School No. 1) from 1930 until 1935, Liceul de Băieți No. 2 în Cernăuți (Boys' Secondary School No. 2 in Cernăuți) from 1935 to 1936,[[4]](#cite_note-4) followed by the Liceul Marele Voievod Mihai (Great Prince Mihai Preparatory School, now Chernivtsi School No. 5), where he studied from 1936 until graduating in 1938.[[5]](#cite_note-5) At this time Celan secretly began to write poetry.

In 1938, Celan traveled to [Tours](/wiki/Tours "Tours"), France, to study [medicine](/wiki/Medicine "Medicine");[[6]](#cite_note-6) the [Anschluss](/wiki/Anschluss "Anschluss") precluded his study in Vienna, and Romanian schools were harder to get into due to the newly imposed [Jewish quota](/wiki/Jewish_quota "Jewish quota"). His journey to France took him through Berlin as the events of *[Kristallnacht](/wiki/Kristallnacht "Kristallnacht")* unfolded, and also introduced him to his uncle, Bruno Schrager, who was later among the French detainees murdered at [Birkenau](/wiki/Auschwitz_concentration_camp#Auschwitz_II_.28Birkenau.29 "Auschwitz concentration camp"). Celan returned to Cernăuți in 1939 to study [literature](/wiki/Literature "Literature") and [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages").[[3]](#cite_note-google.com-3)

### Life during World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Celan&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Life during World War II")]

Following the [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") occupation of Bukovina in June 1940, deportations to [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia") started. A year later, following the reconquest by Romania, [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and the then-fascist Romanian regime brought [ghettos](/wiki/Ghetto "Ghetto"), internment, and [forced labour](/wiki/Forced_labour_under_German_rule_during_World_War_II "Forced labour under German rule during World War II") (see [Romania in World War II](/wiki/Romania_in_World_War_II "Romania in World War II")).

On arrival in Cernăuți in July 1941, the German [SS](/wiki/Schutzstaffel "Schutzstaffel") *[Einsatzkommando](/wiki/Einsatzkommando "Einsatzkommando")* and their Romanian allies set the city's [Great Synagogue](/wiki/Czernowitz_Synagogue "Czernowitz Synagogue") on fire. In October, the Romanians deported a large number of Jews after forcing them into a ghetto, where Celan translated [Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare")'s [sonnets](/wiki/Shakespeare%27s_sonnets "Shakespeare's sonnets") and continued to write his own poetry. Before the ghetto was dissolved in the fall of that year, Celan was pressed into labor, first clearing the debris of a demolished post office, and then gathering and destroying [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") books.[[3]](#cite_note-google.com-3)

The local mayor, [Traian Popovici](/wiki/Traian_Popovici "Traian Popovici"), strove to mitigate the harsh circumstances, until the governor of Bukovina had the Jews rounded up and deported, starting on a Saturday night in June 1942. Celan hoped to convince his parents to leave the country so as to escape certain persecution. While Celan was away from home, on 21 June 1942, his parents were taken from their home and sent by train to an [internment camp](/wiki/Internment_camp "Internment camp") in [Transnistria Governorate](/wiki/Transnistria_Governorate "Transnistria Governorate"), where two-thirds of the deportees eventually perished. Celan's father likely perished of [typhus](/wiki/Typhus "Typhus") and his mother was shot after being exhausted by forced labour. Later that year, after being taken to a labour camp in [Romania](/wiki/Romanian_Old_Kingdom "Romanian Old Kingdom"), Celan received reports of his parents' deaths.[[3]](#cite_note-google.com-3)

Celan remained imprisoned in a work camp until February 1944, when the Red Army's advance forced the Romanians to abandon the camps, whereupon he returned to Cernăuți shortly before the Soviets returned. There, he worked briefly as a nurse in the mental hospital. Friends from this period recall Celan expressing immense guilt over his separation from his parents, whom he had tried to convince to go into hiding prior to the deportations, shortly before their deaths.

### Life after the war

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Celan&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Life after the war")]

Considering emigration to Palestine, Celan left Cernăuți in 1945 for Bucharest, where he remained until 1947. He was active in the Jewish literary community as both a translator of [Russian literature](/wiki/Russian_literature "Russian literature") into Romanian, and as a poet, publishing his work under a variety of pseudonyms. The literary scene of the time was richly populated with [surrealists](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism"), such as [Gellu Naum](/wiki/Gellu_Naum "Gellu Naum"), [Ilarie Voronca](/wiki/Ilarie_Voronca "Ilarie Voronca"), [Gherasim Luca](/wiki/Gherasim_Luca "Gherasim Luca"), [Paul Păun](/wiki/Paul_P%C4%83un "Paul Păun"), and [Dolfi Trost](/wiki/Dolfi_Trost "Dolfi Trost"). It was in this period that Celan developed pseudonyms both for himself and his friends, including the one he took as his pen name. He also met with the poets [Rose Ausländer](/wiki/Rose_Ausl%C3%A4nder "Rose Ausländer") and [Immanuel Weissglas](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Immanuel_Weissglas "de:Immanuel Weissglas"), elements of whose works he reused in his poem "[Todesfuge](/wiki/Todesfuge "Todesfuge")", which first appeared as "*Tangoul Morții*" ("Death [Tango](/wiki/Tango_music "Tango music")") in a Romanian translation of May 1947.[[3]](#cite_note-google.com-3)

### Emigration and Paris years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Celan&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Emigration and Paris years")]

Upon the emergence of the [communist regime in Romania](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Romania "Socialist Republic of Romania") at the beginning of 1948, Celan fled Romania for Vienna, Austria. It was there that he befriended [Ingeborg Bachmann](/wiki/Ingeborg_Bachmann "Ingeborg Bachmann"), who had just completed a dissertation on [Martin Heidegger](/wiki/Martin_Heidegger "Martin Heidegger"). Celan, however, found only a ruined city divided between Allied powers and which bore little resemblance to the literary, musical, and cultural mecca it had been as the capital of the [Austro-Hungarian Empire](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Empire "Austro-Hungarian Empire"). Furthermore, the urbane, cultured, and sophisticated [Viennese Jewish](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Vienna "History of the Jews in Vienna") community described by [Stefan Zweig](/wiki/Stefan_Zweig "Stefan Zweig") in *[The World of Yesterday](/wiki/The_World_of_Yesterday "The World of Yesterday")* had been largely annihilated by the [Holocaust in Austria](/wiki/Holocaust_in_Austria "Holocaust in Austria"). This is why, like the poet [Heinrich Heine](/wiki/Heinrich_Heine "Heinrich Heine") before him, Celan emigrated to Paris in 1948. In that year his first poetry collection, *[Der Sand aus den Urnen](/wiki/Der_Sand_aus_den_Urnen "Der Sand aus den Urnen")* ("Sand from the Urns"), was published in Vienna by A. Sexl. His first few years in Paris were marked by intense feelings of loneliness and isolation, as expressed in letters to his colleagues, including his longtime friend from Cernăuți, Petre Solomon. It was also during this time that he exchanged many letters with Diet Kloos, a young singer and anti-Nazi [Dutch Resistance](/wiki/Dutch_Resistance "Dutch Resistance") veteran who had witnessed her husband of just a few months being tortured to death. She visited Celan twice in Paris between 1949 and 1951.[[3]](#cite_note-google.com-3) During his stay in Paris, he became friends with [Emil Cioran](/wiki/Emil_Cioran "Emil Cioran") and [Serge Moscovici](/wiki/Serge_Moscovici "Serge Moscovici").

In 1952, Celan's writing began to gain recognition when he read his poetry on his first reading trip to [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany")[[7]](#cite_note-7) where he was invited to read at the semiannual meetings of the hugely influential [Group 47](/wiki/Group_47 "Group 47") literary group.[[8]](#cite_note-8) At their May meeting he read his poem *[Todesfuge](/wiki/Todesfuge "Todesfuge")* ("Death Fugue"), a depiction of concentration camp life. When Ingeborg Bachmann, with whom Celan had an affair, won the group's prize for her poetry collection **Die gestundete Zeit** (*The Extended Hours*), Celan (whose work had received only six votes) said "After the meeting, only six people remembered my name".[*[This quote needs a citation](/wiki/Wikipedia:Inline_citation#When_you_must_use_inline_citations "Wikipedia:Inline citation")*] He did not attend any other meeting of the group.[[3]](#cite_note-google.com-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Grave-Paul-Celan.jpg/250px-Grave-Paul-Celan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grave-Paul-Celan.jpg)

The grave of Paul Celan at the [Cimetière de Thiais](/wiki/Cimeti%C3%A8re_de_Thiais "Cimetière de Thiais") near Paris

In November 1951, he met the [graphic artist](/wiki/Graphic_artist "Graphic artist") [Gisèle Lestrange](/wiki/Gis%C3%A8le_Lestrange "Gisèle Lestrange"), in Paris. He sent her many love letters, influenced by [Franz Kafka](/wiki/Franz_Kafka "Franz Kafka")'s correspondence with [Milena Jesenská](/wiki/Milena_Jesensk%C3%A1 "Milena Jesenská") and [Felice Bauer](/wiki/Felice_Bauer "Felice Bauer").[[9]](#cite_note-9) They married on 21 December 1952, despite the opposition of her aristocratic family. During the following 18 years they wrote over 700 letters; Celan's active correspondents also included [Hermann Lenz](/wiki/Hermann_Lenz "Hermann Lenz") and his wife Hanne.[[10]](#cite_note-10) He made his living as a translator and lecturer in German at the [École normale supérieure](/wiki/%C3%89cole_normale_sup%C3%A9rieure_(Paris) "École normale supérieure (Paris)"). He was a close friend of [Nelly Sachs](/wiki/Nelly_Sachs "Nelly Sachs"), who later won the Nobel Prize for literature.[[3]](#cite_note-google.com-3)

Celan became a French citizen in 1955 and lived in Paris. Celan's sense of persecution increased after the widow of a friend, the French-German poet [Yvan Goll](/wiki/Yvan_Goll "Yvan Goll"), unjustly accused him of having plagiarised her husband's work.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Celan was awarded the [Bremen Literature Prize](/wiki/Literaturpreis_der_Stadt_Bremen "Literaturpreis der Stadt Bremen") in 1958 and the [Georg Büchner Prize](/wiki/Georg_B%C3%BCchner_Prize "Georg Büchner Prize") in 1960.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[3]](#cite_note-google.com-3)

Celan drowned in the river [Seine](/wiki/Seine "Seine") in Paris around 20 April 1970.[[14]](#cite_note-14) It may have been suicide, and if so, perhaps related to the appearance of Weissglas's poem, dated 1944, in the Romanian journal *Neue Literatur*, and fears that he might again be accused unfairly of plagiarism, the initial assertions about which, in 1953, later occasioned four psychotic episodes involving paranoia.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Poetic style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Celan&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Poetic style")]

In addition to writing poetry (in German and, earlier, in Romanian), he was an extremely active translator and [polyglot](/wiki/Polyglot "Polyglot"), translating literature from Romanian, French, Spanish, Portuguese, Italian, Russian, Hebrew, and English into German. Meanwhile, Celan's own poetry became progressively more cryptic, fractured and monosyllabic, often deviating from conventional poetic meter and verse structures. He created and used German [neologisms](/wiki/Neologism "Neologism"), especially in his later works *[Fadensonnen](/wiki/Fadensonnen "Fadensonnen")* ("Threadsuns") and *[Lichtzwang](/wiki/Lichtzwang "Lichtzwang")*. Celan has been seen as attempting either to destroy or remake the German language in his poetry, using it to convey dense imagery and subjective experiences; he described this stance in a letter to his wife Gisèle Lestrange as feeling as though "the German I talk is not the same as the language the German people are talking here".

The death of his parents and the trauma of [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust") are regarded by scholars as being defining forces in Celan's poetry and his use of language. In his Bremen Prize speech, Celan said of language after [Auschwitz](/wiki/Auschwitz_concentration_camp "Auschwitz concentration camp") that:

> Only one thing remained reachable, close and secure amid all losses: language. Yes, language. In spite of everything, it remained secure against loss. But it had to go through its own lack of answers, through terrifying silence, through the thousand darknesses of murderous speech. It went through. It gave me no words for what was happening, but went through it. Went through and could resurface, 'enriched' by it all.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Celan also said: "There is nothing in the world for which a poet will give up writing, not even when he is a Jew and the language of his poems is German."[[17]](#cite_note-17)

His masterpiece, "Todesfuge", may have drawn some key motifs from the poem "ER" by his fellow Romanian poet Immanuel Weissglas, another Czernovitz poet.[[18]](#cite_note-Rostagno-18) The characters of Margarete and Sulamith, with their respectively golden and ashen hair, can be interpreted as a reflection of Celan's Jewish-German culture,[[18]](#cite_note-Rostagno-18) while the blue-eyed "Master from Germany" embodies German Nazism.

## Significance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Celan&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Significance")]

Philosophers including [Maurice Blanchot](/wiki/Maurice_Blanchot "Maurice Blanchot"), [Jacques Derrida](/wiki/Jacques_Derrida "Jacques Derrida") and [Hans-Georg Gadamer](/wiki/Hans-Georg_Gadamer "Hans-Georg Gadamer") devoted at least one of their books to the [poetics](/wiki/Poetics "Poetics") of Celan's work.[[19]](#cite_note-19) He has been regarded, alongside [Goethe](/wiki/Johann_Wolfgang_von_Goethe "Johann Wolfgang von Goethe"), [Hölderlin](/wiki/Friedrich_H%C3%B6lderlin "Friedrich Hölderlin") and [Rilke](/wiki/Rainer_Maria_Rilke "Rainer Maria Rilke"), as one of the most significant German poets, and a radical innovator of [German-language literature](/wiki/German-language_literature "German-language literature").[[20]](#cite_note-20) Despite the difficulty of his work, his poetry is thoroughly researched, with the total number of scholarly papers numbering in the thousands.

## In film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Celan&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: In film")]

*[The Dreamed Ones](/w/index.php?title=The_Dreamed_Ones&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Dreamed Ones (page does not exist)")* (*Die Geträumten*; 2016), is a feature film based on the almost 20-year correspondence between Celan and poet [Ingeborg Bachmann](/wiki/Ingeborg_Bachmann "Ingeborg Bachmann").[[21]](#cite_note-21) It was directed by [Ruth Beckermann](/wiki/Ruth_Beckermann "Ruth Beckermann"), and won several awards.[[22]](#cite_note-22)
